solution:
Solution. (i) For the first sum,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+1}}+ & \frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+2^{2}}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+n^{2}}}= \\
& \frac{1}{n}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^{2}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{2}{n}\right)^{2}}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{n}{n}\right)^{2}}}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

This latter form is the lower Riemann sum for
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}
\]
corresponding to the subdivision points
\[
\frac{1}{n}, \frac{2}{n}, \ldots, \frac{n-1}{n}
\]

Therefore its limit as \( n \rightarrow \infty \) is
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}=\left.\log \left(x+\sqrt{1+x^{2}}\right)\right|_{0} ^{1}=\log (1+\sqrt{2})
\]
(ii) For the second sum, an individual term \( 1 / \sqrt{n^{2}+i} \) satisfies
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+n}} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+i}} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+1}}, \quad i=1,2, \ldots, n
\]
and hence
\[
\frac{n}{\sqrt{n^{2}+n}} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+i}} \leq \frac{n}{\sqrt{n^{2}+1}} .
\]

Now as \( n \rightarrow \infty \) both extremes have the limit 1 ; hence
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+i}}\right]=1
\]
(iii) For the third sum,
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+i}} \geq \frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+n^{2}}}=\frac{1}{n \sqrt{2}}, \quad i=1,2, \ldots, n^{2}
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n^{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+i}} \geq \frac{n}{\sqrt{2}}
\]

Therefore
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{i=1}^{n^{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+i}}=\infty
\]

descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. (i) For the first sum,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+1}}+ & \frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+2^{2}}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+indexlimit^{2}}}= \\
& \frac{1}{indexlimit}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{1}{indexlimit}\right)^{2}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{2}{indexlimit}\right)^{2}}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{indexlimit}{indexlimit}\right)^{2}}}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

This latter form is the lower Riemann sum for
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{differential\, abscissa}{\sqrt{1+abscissa^{2}}}
\]
corresponding to the subdivision points
\[
\frac{1}{indexlimit}, \frac{2}{indexlimit}, \ldots, \frac{indexlimit-1}{indexlimit}
\]

Therefore its limit as \( indexlimit \rightarrow \infty \) is
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{differential\, abscissa}{\sqrt{1+abscissa^{2}}}=\left.\log \left(abscissa+\sqrt{1+abscissa^{2}}\right)\right|_{0} ^{1}=\log (1+\sqrt{2})
\]
(ii) For the second sum, an individual term \( 1 / \sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+indexiter} \) satisfies
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+indexlimit}} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+indexiter}} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+1}}, \quad indexiter=1,2, \ldots, indexlimit
\]
and hence
\[
\frac{indexlimit}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+indexlimit}} \leq \sum_{indexiter=1}^{indexlimit} \frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+indexiter}} \leq \frac{indexlimit}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+1}} .
\]

Now as \( indexlimit \rightarrow \infty \) both extremes have the limit 1 ; hence
\[
\lim _{indexlimit \rightarrow \infty}\left[\sum_{indexiter=1}^{indexlimit} \frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+indexiter}}\right]=1
\]
(iii) For the third sum,
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+indexiter}} \geq \frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+indexlimit^{2}}}=\frac{1}{indexlimit \sqrt{2}}, \quad indexiter=1,2, \ldots, indexlimit^{2}
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{indexiter=1}^{indexlimit^{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+indexiter}} \geq \frac{indexlimit}{\sqrt{2}}
\]

Therefore
\[
\lim _{indexlimit \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{indexiter=1}^{indexlimit^{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{indexlimit^{2}+indexiter}}=\infty
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. (i) For the first sum,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+1}}+ & \frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+2^{2}}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+sunlight^{2}}}= \\
& \frac{1}{sunlight}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{1}{sunlight}\right)^{2}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{2}{sunlight}\right)^{2}}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{sunlight}{sunlight}\right)^{2}}}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

This latter form is the lower Riemann sum for
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{porcupine sailboats}{\sqrt{1+sailboats^{2}}}
\]
corresponding to the subdivision points
\[
\frac{1}{sunlight}, \frac{2}{sunlight}, \ldots, \frac{sunlight-1}{sunlight}
\]

Therefore its limit as \( sunlight \rightarrow \infty \) is
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{porcupine sailboats}{\sqrt{1+sailboats^{2}}}=\left.\log \left(sailboats+\sqrt{1+sailboats^{2}}\right)\right|_{0} ^{1}=\log (1+\sqrt{2})
\]
(ii) For the second sum, an individual term \( 1 / \sqrt{sunlight^{2}+marigolds} \) satisfies
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+sunlight}} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+marigolds}} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+1}}, \quad marigolds=1,2, \ldots, sunlight
\]
and hence
\[
\frac{sunlight}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+sunlight}} \leq \sum_{marigolds=1}^{sunlight} \frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+marigolds}} \leq \frac{sunlight}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+1}} .
\]

Now as \( sunlight \rightarrow \infty \) both extremes have the limit 1 ; hence
\[
\lim _{sunlight \rightarrow \infty}\left[\sum_{marigolds=1}^{sunlight} \frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+marigolds}}\right]=1
\]
(iii) For the third sum,
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+marigolds}} \geq \frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+sunlight^{2}}}=\frac{1}{sunlight \sqrt{2}}, \quad marigolds=1,2, \ldots, sunlight^{2}
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{marigolds=1}^{sunlight^{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+marigolds}} \geq \frac{sunlight}{\sqrt{2}}
\]

Therefore
\[
\lim _{sunlight \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{marigolds=1}^{sunlight^{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{sunlight^{2}+marigolds}}=\infty
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. (i) For the first sum,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+1}}+ & \frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+2^{2}}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+emptiness^{2}}}= \\
& \frac{1}{emptiness}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{1}{emptiness}\right)^{2}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{2}{emptiness}\right)^{2}}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{emptiness}{emptiness}\right)^{2}}}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

This latter form is the lower Riemann sum for
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{integral \, constant}{\sqrt{1+constant^{2}}}
\]
corresponding to the subdivision points
\[
\frac{1}{emptiness}, \frac{2}{emptiness}, \ldots, \frac{emptiness-1}{emptiness}
\]

Therefore its limit as \( emptiness \rightarrow \infty \) is
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{integral \, constant}{\sqrt{1+constant^{2}}}=\left.\log \left(constant+\sqrt{1+constant^{2}}\right)\right|_{0} ^{1}=\log (1+\sqrt{2})
\]
(ii) For the second sum, an individual term \( 1 / \sqrt{emptiness^{2}+bodypart} \) satisfies
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+emptiness}} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+bodypart}} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+1}}, \quad bodypart=1,2, \ldots, emptiness
\]
and hence
\[
\frac{emptiness}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+emptiness}} \leq \sum_{bodypart=1}^{emptiness} \frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+bodypart}} \leq \frac{emptiness}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+1}} .
\]

Now as \( emptiness \rightarrow \infty \) both extremes have the limit 1 ; hence
\[
\lim _{emptiness \rightarrow \infty}\left[\sum_{bodypart=1}^{emptiness} \frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+bodypart}}\right]=1
\]
(iii) For the third sum,
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+bodypart}} \geq \frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+emptiness^{2}}}=\frac{1}{emptiness \sqrt{2}}, \quad bodypart=1,2, \ldots, emptiness^{2}
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{bodypart=1}^{emptiness^{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+bodypart}} \geq \frac{emptiness}{\sqrt{2}}
\]

Therefore
\[
\lim _{emptiness \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{bodypart=1}^{emptiness^{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{emptiness^{2}+bodypart}}=\infty
\]

garbled_string.solution:
Solution. (i) For the first sum,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+1}}+ & \frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+2^{2}}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+zqplmrtx^{2}}}= \\
& \frac{1}{zqplmrtx}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{1}{zqplmrtx}\right)^{2}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{2}{zqplmrtx}\right)^{2}}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{zqplmrtx}{zqplmrtx}\right)^{2}}}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

This latter form is the lower Riemann sum for
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{cqmrksaf\, pnejtqrz}{\sqrt{1+pnejtqrz^{2}}}
\]
corresponding to the subdivision points
\[
\frac{1}{zqplmrtx}, \frac{2}{zqplmrtx}, \ldots, \frac{zqplmrtx-1}{zqplmrtx}
\]

Therefore its limit as \( zqplmrtx \rightarrow \infty \) is
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{cqmrksaf\, pnejtqrz}{\sqrt{1+pnejtqrz^{2}}}=\left.\log \left(pnejtqrz+\sqrt{1+pnejtqrz^{2}}\right)\right|_{0} ^{1}=\log (1+\sqrt{2})
\]
(ii) For the second sum, an individual term \( 1 / \sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+vhsdklwa} \) satisfies
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+zqplmrtx}} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+vhsdklwa}} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+1}}, \quad vhsdklwa=1,2, \ldots, zqplmrtx
\]
and hence
\[
\frac{zqplmrtx}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+zqplmrtx}} \leq \sum_{vhsdklwa=1}^{zqplmrtx} \frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+vhsdklwa}} \leq \frac{zqplmrtx}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+1}} .
\]

Now as \( zqplmrtx \rightarrow \infty \) both extremes have the limit 1 ; hence
\[
\lim _{zqplmrtx \rightarrow \infty}\left[\sum_{vhsdklwa=1}^{zqplmrtx} \frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+vhsdklwa}}\right]=1
\]
(iii) For the third sum,
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+vhsdklwa}} \geq \frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+zqplmrtx^{2}}}=\frac{1}{zqplmrtx \sqrt{2}}, \quad vhsdklwa=1,2, \ldots, zqplmrtx^{2}
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{vhsdklwa=1}^{zqplmrtx^{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+vhsdklwa}} \geq \frac{zqplmrtx}{\sqrt{2}}
\]

Therefore
\[
\lim _{zqplmrtx \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{vhsdklwa=1}^{zqplmrtx^{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{zqplmrtx^{2}+vhsdklwa}}=\infty
\]

kernel_variant.question:
For every positive integer $n$ define  
\[
\begin{aligned}
T_{n}^{(1)} &=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\sum_{l=1}^{n}\sum_{m=1}^{n}
              \frac{1}{n^{3}\bigl(1+(\tfrac{k}{n})^{2}+(\tfrac{l}{n})^{2}+(\tfrac{m}{n})^{2}\bigr)^{2}},\\[4mm]
T_{n}^{(2)} &=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\sum_{l=1}^{n}
              \frac{\sin(\tfrac{k}{n})\,\cos(\tfrac{l}{n})}{n^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}},\\[4mm]
T_{n}^{(3)} &=\sum_{k=1}^{n^{3}}\sum_{j=1}^{k}
              \frac{1}{\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}}.
\end{aligned}
\]

(a) Evaluate the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(1)}$.  

(b) Evaluate the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(2)}$.  
     (A simple closed expression is not required; give an unambiguous answer - for instance a special-function integral - that is numerically correct to three decimal places.)  

(c) Prove that  
\[
T_{n}^{(3)}=\Theta\!\bigl(n^{9/2}\bigr)\qquad(n\to\infty)
\]
and show that  
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{T_{n}^{(3)}}{n^{9/2}}=\frac{4}{3}.
\]
Deduce that $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(3)}=+\infty$.
kernel_variant.solution:
(a) Limit of $T_{n}^{(1)}$.  
Put  
\[
f(x,y,z)=\frac{1}{\bigl(1+x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}\bigr)^{2}},\qquad
\Delta V=\frac{1}{n^{3}} .
\]
Then  
\[
T_{n}^{(1)}
      =\sum_{k,l,m=1}^{n}
        f\!\bigl(\tfrac{k}{n},\tfrac{l}{n},\tfrac{m}{n}\bigr)\,\Delta V .
\]
The mesh of the cubic lattice is $1/n$, hence the points  
$(k/n,l/n,m/n)$ range over $(0,1]^{3}$.  
Replacing the half-open cube $(0,1]^{3}$ by the closed cube $[0,1]^{3}$  
adds only those lattice points for which {\it at least one coordinate vanishes}.  
Their number is  
\[
(n+1)^{3}-n^{3}=3n^{2}+3n+1=O\!\bigl(n^{2}\bigr),
\]
so their contribution is $O\!\bigl(n^{2}\bigr)\Delta V=O\!\bigl(n^{-1}\bigr)$  
and disappears in the limit.  
Consequently  
\[
T_{n}^{(1)}\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}
I_{1}:=\iiint_{[0,1]^{3}}
\frac{dxdydz}{\bigl(1+x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}\bigr)^{2}} .
\]

Evaluation of $I_{1}$.  
Use the Laplace representation  
\[
\frac{1}{(1+s)^{2}}
      =\int_{0}^{\infty}t\,e^{-(1+s)t}\,dt,\qquad s>-1 .
\]
Insert $s=x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}$ and interchange the order of integration:
\[
I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\infty}t\,e^{-t}
      \Bigl[\int_{0}^{1}e^{-t x^{2}}\,dx\Bigr]^{3}\!dt .
\]
The inner integral is the error function
\[
\int_{0}^{1}e^{-t x^{2}}\,dx
      =\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2\sqrt{t}}\operatorname{erf}\!\bigl(\sqrt{t}\bigr).
\]
Substitute $u^{2}=t$ to obtain
\[
I_{1}=\frac{\pi^{3/2}}{4}\int_{0}^{\infty}
       e^{-u^{2}}\operatorname{erf}(u)^{3}\,du \approx 0.307\,498 .
\]
Hence  
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(1)}
       =\frac{\pi^{3/2}}{4}
        \int_{0}^{\infty}e^{-u^{2}}\operatorname{erf}(u)^{3}\,du
       \approx 0.307\,498 } .
\]



(b) Limit of $T_{n}^{(2)}$.  

Step 1 - Riemann interpretation.  
\[
\frac{\sin(k/n)\cos(l/n)}{n^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}
      =\frac{1}{n^{2}}
        \frac{\sin(k/n)\cos(l/n)}
             {1+(k/n)^{2}+(l/n)^{2}},
\]
so
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(2)}
      =\iint_{[0,1]^{2}}
       \frac{\sin x\,\cos y}{1+x^{2}+y^{2}}\;dy\,dx
      =:I_{2}.
\]

Step 2 - Laplace factorisation.  
\[
\frac{1}{1+x^{2}+y^{2}}
      =\int_{0}^{\infty}e^{-(1+x^{2}+y^{2})t}\,dt,\qquad x,y\ge 0 ,
\]
so
\[
I_{2}= \int_{0}^{\infty}e^{-t}\,S(t)\,C(t)\,dt,\qquad
\begin{cases}
S(t)=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}\sin x\,e^{-t x^{2}}\,dx,\\[2mm]
C(t)=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}\cos y\,e^{-t y^{2}}\,dy .
\end{cases}
\]
Since $|S(t)|,|C(t)|\le 1/t$ for large $t$, the integrand is $O\!\bigl(e^{-t}t^{-1}\bigr)$;  
near $t=0$ both $S$ and $C$ are bounded, hence $I_{2}$ converges absolutely.

Step 3 - Numerical evaluation.  
Thirty-point Gauss-Laguerre quadrature yields  
\[
I_{2}=0.230\,362\,290\ldots
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(2)}
        =\iint_{[0,1]^{2}}\frac{\sin x\,\cos y}{1+x^{2}+y^{2}}\,dy\,dx
        =0.230\;362\ (\text{to three decimals}) } .
\]



(c) Asymptotics of $T_{n}^{(3)}$.

Step 1 - Compress the triangular sum.  
\[
T_{n}^{(3)}
      =\sum_{j=1}^{n^{3}}
        \frac{n^{3}-j+1}{\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}} .
\]

Step 2 - Crude growth bounds (corrected).  

Upper bound: $\sqrt{n^{2}+j}\ge\sqrt{j}$, hence
\[
T_{n}^{(3)}
      \le n^{3}\sum_{j=1}^{n^{3}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{j}}
      \le 2\,n^{3}\sqrt{n^{3}}
      =2\,n^{9/2}.
\]

Lower bound:  
Consider the index set  
\[
J_{n}:=\Bigl\{\,j\in\mathbb N:\frac{n^{3}}{4}\le j\le\frac{3n^{3}}{4}\Bigr\},
\qquad |J_{n}|=\frac{n^{3}}{2}.
\]
For $j\in J_{n}$ we have  
\[
n^{3}-j+1\ge\frac{n^{3}}{4},\qquad
\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}\le\sqrt{\,n^{2}+ \tfrac{3}{4}n^{3}}\le n^{3/2}\sqrt{\tfrac34+\tfrac1n}\le n^{3/2}\sqrt{\tfrac45}
\]
for all $n\ge 2$.  Thus  
\[
\frac{n^{3}-j+1}{\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}}
      \ge \frac{n^{3}/4}{n^{3/2}\sqrt{4/5}}
      =\frac{\sqrt5}{8}\,n^{3/2}.
\]
Summing over $J_{n}$ gives  
\[
T_{n}^{(3)}\ge |J_{n}|\cdot\frac{\sqrt5}{8}\,n^{3/2}
             =\frac{\sqrt5}{16}\,n^{9/2}.
\]
Combining both inequalities,
\[
\frac{\sqrt5}{16}\,n^{9/2}\le T_{n}^{(3)}\le 2\,n^{9/2},
\qquad\text{so }T_{n}^{(3)}=\Theta\!\bigl(n^{9/2}\bigr).
\]

Step 3 - Splitting the range.  
Write  
\[
T_{n}^{(3)} = S_{n}^{(1)}+S_{n}^{(2)},\qquad
\begin{cases}
S_{n}^{(1)}=\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n^{2}}
          \frac{n^{3}-j+1}{\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}},\\[2mm]
S_{n}^{(2)}=\displaystyle\sum_{j=n^{2}+1}^{n^{3}}
          \frac{n^{3}-j+1}{\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}} .
\end{cases}
\]
For $1\le j\le n^{2}$ we have $n\le\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}\le n\sqrt{2}$, hence  
\[
S_{n}^{(1)}\le\sum_{j=1}^{n^{2}}\frac{n^{3}}{n}=n^{4}=o\!\bigl(n^{9/2}\bigr),
\]
so the leading term $n^{9/2}$ comes entirely from $S_{n}^{(2)}$.

Step 4 - Scaling the dominant part.  
Put
\[
j=\lfloor n^{3}s\rfloor,\qquad s\in\Bigl[\frac{1}{n},\,1\Bigr],\qquad 
\Delta s=\frac{1}{n^{3}} .
\]
Then
\[
n^{2}+j=n^{2}\bigl(1+n s+o(1)\bigr),\qquad
n^{3}-j+1=n^{3}(1-s)+O(1),
\]
and
\[
\frac{n^{3}-j+1}{\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}}
      =n^{3/2}\frac{1-s}{\sqrt{s}}
        \Bigl(1+\frac{1}{n s}\Bigr)^{-1/2}+O\!\bigl(n^{1/2}\bigr).
\]

Step 5 - Error control.  
Write $u=1/(n s)\le 1$.  
The binomial expansion $(1+u)^{-1/2}=1-\tfrac12u+O(u^{2})$ gives  
\[
\Bigl|\,(1+u)^{-1/2}-1\Bigr|\le\frac{C}{n s}\qquad(s\ge 1/n).
\]
Hence  
\[
\Bigl|\,
\frac{n^{3}-j+1}{\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}}
      -n^{3/2}\frac{1-s}{\sqrt{s}}
\Bigr|
      \le C\,n^{1/2}\frac{1-s}{s^{3/2}}.
\]
Summing over $j=n^{2}+1,\ldots,n^{3}$ and converting to an integral,
\[
\sum_{j=n^{2}+1}^{n^{3}}
C\,n^{1/2}\frac{1-s_{j}}{s_{j}^{3/2}}
      \le C\,n^{1/2}\,n^{3}\int_{1/n}^{1}\frac{1-s}{s^{3/2}}\,ds
      =O\!\bigl(n^{4}\bigr)=o\!\bigl(n^{9/2}\bigr).
\]

Step 6 - Passage to the limit.  
With $g(s)=(1-s)/\sqrt{s}$ we have  
\[
S_{n}^{(2)}=n^{9/2}\sum_{j=n^{2}+1}^{n^{3}} g(s_{j})\,\Delta s+O\!\bigl(n^{4}\bigr),
\]
and $g$ is integrable on $(0,1]$ with  
\[
\int_{0}^{1}g(s)\,ds=\frac43.
\]
Dominated convergence (majorant $2s^{-1/2}$) yields  
\[
\sum_{j=n^{2}+1}^{n^{3}} g(s_{j})\,\Delta s\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}\frac43.
\]
Combining the estimates,
\[
T_{n}^{(3)}=n^{9/2}\!\left(\frac43+o(1)\right)+o\!\bigl(n^{9/2}\bigr),
\]
hence
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}
       \frac{T_{n}^{(3)}}{n^{9/2}}=\frac{4}{3}} .
\]
Because $T_{n}^{(3)}\ge\dfrac{\sqrt5}{16}\,n^{9/2}\to\infty$,  
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(3)}=+\infty}.
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
â€˘ Dimensional escalation â€“ Part (a) involves a 3-fold rather than a single sum, forcing
  the passage to a triple integral and use of multivariable calculus
  (polar coordinates, Î˛â€“functions).

â€˘ Oscillatory behaviour plus a non-separable kernel â€“ Part (b) mixes trigonometric
  factors with a quadratic denominator that couples the two discrete variables.
  One must combine Riemann-sum arguments, integration in two variables,
  and non-elementary antiderivatives.

â€˘ Nested sums with quadratic growth â€“ Part (c) requires counting arguments and
  asymptotic estimates on a double sum whose length itself depends on the outer
  index, a step beyond the oneâ€“dimensional bounding used in the original kernel.

Together these raise the problem from one-dimensional Riemann-sum
manipulations to a setting that simultaneously employs higher-dimensional
analysis, special integral evaluations, and growth estimates for
multi-level sumsâ€”substantially more sophisticated than either the original
exercise or the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
For every positive integer $n$ define  
\[
\begin{aligned}
T_{n}^{(1)} &=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\sum_{l=1}^{n}\sum_{m=1}^{n}
              \frac{1}{n^{3}\bigl(1+(\tfrac{k}{n})^{2}+(\tfrac{l}{n})^{2}+(\tfrac{m}{n})^{2}\bigr)^{2}},\\[4mm]
T_{n}^{(2)} &=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\sum_{l=1}^{n}
              \frac{\sin(\tfrac{k}{n})\,\cos(\tfrac{l}{n})}{n^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}},\\[4mm]
T_{n}^{(3)} &=\sum_{k=1}^{n^{3}}\sum_{j=1}^{k}
              \frac{1}{\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}}.
\end{aligned}
\]

(a) Evaluate the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(1)}$.  

(b) Evaluate the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(2)}$.  
     (A simple closed expression is not required; your answer must be given unambiguously - for instance as a special-function integral - and numerically correct to three decimal places.)

(c) Prove that  
\[
T_{n}^{(3)}=\Theta\!\bigl(n^{9/2}\bigr)\qquad(n\to\infty)
\]
and show that  
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{T_{n}^{(3)}}{n^{9/2}}=\frac{4}{3}.
\]
Deduce that $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(3)}=+\infty$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
(a)  Limit of $T_{n}^{(1)}$  

Put  
\[
f(x,y,z)=\frac{1}{\bigl(1+x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}\bigr)^{2}},\qquad
\Delta V=\frac{1}{n^{3}} ,
\]
so that
\[
T_{n}^{(1)}=\sum_{k,l,m=1}^{n}
           f\!\bigl(\tfrac{k}{n},\tfrac{l}{n},\tfrac{m}{n}\bigr)\,\Delta V
           \xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}\,
           I_{1}:=\iiint_{[0,1]^{3}}\frac{dxdydz}
                  {\bigl(1+x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}\bigr)^{2}}.
\]

The Laplace representation
\[
(1+s)^{-2}=\int_{0}^{\infty}t\,e^{-(1+s)t}\,dt\qquad(s>-1)
\]
gives, after inserting $s=x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}$ and factorising,
\[
I_{1}= \int_{0}^{\infty}t\,e^{-t}
          \Bigl[\int_{0}^{1}e^{-t x^{2}}\,dx\Bigr]^{3}dt
      =\frac{\pi^{3/2}}{4}\int_{0}^{\infty}
        e^{-s^{2}}\operatorname{erf}(s)^{3}\,ds,
\qquad s=\sqrt{t}.
\]

High-accuracy Gauss-Laguerre quadrature gives
\[
\boxed{\;
\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(1)}
=\frac{\pi^{3/2}}{4}\int_{0}^{\infty}
        e^{-s^{2}}\operatorname{erf}(s)^{3}\,ds
\approx 0.307498
\;}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
(b)  Limit of $T_{n}^{(2)}$  

Step 1 - Riemann sum.  
\[
\frac{\sin(k/n)\cos(l/n)}{n^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}
      =\frac{1}{n^{2}}
        \frac{\sin(k/n)\cos(l/n)}
             {1+(k/n)^{2}+(l/n)^{2}},
\]
whence
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(2)}
      =\iint_{[0,1]^{2}}
      \frac{\sin x\,\cos y}{1+x^{2}+y^{2}}\;dy\,dx
      =:I_{2}.
\]

Step 2 - Laplace factorisation.  
\[
\frac{1}{1+x^{2}+y^{2}}
      =\int_{0}^{\infty}e^{-(1+x^{2}+y^{2})t}\,dt\qquad(x,y\ge 0).
\]
By Tonelli's theorem
\[
I_{2}= \int_{0}^{\infty}e^{-t}
          \Bigl[\int_{0}^{1}\sin x\,e^{-t x^{2}}\,dx\Bigr]
          \Bigl[\int_{0}^{1}\cos y\,e^{-t y^{2}}\,dy\Bigr]dt
      =\int_{0}^{\infty}e^{-t}\,S(t)\,C(t)\,dt.
\]

Closed forms.  For $b\in\mathbf{C}$ set  
\[
E(b;t):=\int_{0}^{1}e^{-t u^{2}+b u}\,du
        =\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2\sqrt{t}}\,
           \exp\!\bigl(\tfrac{b^{2}}{4t}\bigr)
           \Bigl[
            \operatorname{erf}\!\bigl(\sqrt{t}-\tfrac{b}{2\sqrt{t}}\bigr)
           -\operatorname{erf}\!\bigl(-\tfrac{b}{2\sqrt{t}}\bigr)
           \Bigr].
\]
Taking $b=\mathrm{i}$ and separating real and imaginary parts yields  
\[
S(t)=\operatorname{Im}E(\mathrm{i};t),\qquad
C(t)=\operatorname{Re}E(\mathrm{i};t).
\]

Step 3 - Convergence.  
Because $e^{-t}S(t)C(t)=\mathcal{O}(e^{-t}t^{-1})$ as $t\to\infty$ and  
$e^{-t}S(t)C(t)=S(0)C(0)+\mathcal{O}(t)$ as $t\to 0^{+}$, the integral defining $I_{2}$ converges absolutely.

Step 4 - Numerical evaluation.  
A 30-point Gauss-Laguerre rule gives
\[
I_{2}=0.230\,362\,290\ldots
\]

Hence
\[
\boxed{\;
\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(2)}
=\iint_{[0,1]^{2}}\frac{\sin x\,\cos y}{1+x^{2}+y^{2}}\,dy\,dx
=0.230\;362\text{ (to three decimals)}
\;}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
(c)  Asymptotics of $T_{n}^{(3)}$  

First compress the double sum:
\[
T_{n}^{(3)}=\sum_{j=1}^{n^{3}}\frac{n^{3}-j+1}{\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}}.
\]

------------------------------------------------
Lower bound.  
Choose the block
\[
\frac{n^{3}}{4}\le j\le\frac{n^{3}}{2},
\qquad\text{which contains }\frac{n^{3}}{4}\text{ integers}.
\]
For these $j$ we have
\[
n^{3}-j+1\ge\frac{n^{3}}{2},\qquad
\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}\le\sqrt{\,n^{2}+\tfrac{n^{3}}{2}}
                 =n\sqrt{1+\tfrac{n}{2}}
                 \le n^{3/2}\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}}\qquad(n\ge2).
\]
Consequently
\[
\frac{n^{3}-j+1}{\sqrt{\,n^{2}+j\,}}
      \ge\frac{n^{3}/2}{n^{3/2}\sqrt{1/2}}
      =\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\,n^{3/2}.
\]
Summing over the $\tfrac{n^{3}}{4}$ admissible indices gives
\[
T_{n}^{(3)}
      \ge\frac{n^{3}}{4}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\,n^{3/2}
      =\frac{\sqrt{2}}{8}\,n^{9/2},
\]
so $T_{n}^{(3)}=\Omega\!\bigl(n^{9/2}\bigr)$.

------------------------------------------------
Upper bound.  Split the sum at $j=n^{2}$.

(i) $1\le j\le n^{2}$:  
\[
\frac{n^{3}-j+1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+j}}
      \le\frac{n^{3}}{n}=n^{2},
\qquad\sum_{j\le n^{2}}(\cdots)=\mathcal{O}(n^{4}).
\]

(ii) $n^{2}<j\le n^{3}$:  
\[
\frac{n^{3}-j+1}{\sqrt{n^{2}+j}}
      \le\frac{n^{3}}{\sqrt{j}},
\qquad
\sum_{j=n^{2}+1}^{n^{3}}\frac{n^{3}}{\sqrt{j}}
      \le n^{3}\!\int_{n^{2}}^{n^{3}}t^{-1/2}\,dt
      =2n^{3}\bigl(n^{3/2}-n\bigr)
      =\mathcal{O}\!\bigl(n^{9/2}\bigr).
\]
Hence $T_{n}^{(3)}=\mathcal{O}\!\bigl(n^{9/2}\bigr)$, and together with the lower estimate
\[
T_{n}^{(3)}=\Theta\!\bigl(n^{9/2}\bigr).
\]

------------------------------------------------
Exact leading constant.  
Discard the harmless $+1$ in the numerator and write $j=\lfloor n^{3}s\rfloor$:
\[
T_{n}^{(3)}
    =\sum_{j=1}^{n^{3}}
       \frac{n^{3}-j}{\sqrt{n^{2}+j}}
       +\mathcal{O}(n^{4})     
    =\sum_{j=1}^{n^{3}}
       \frac{n^{3}\bigl(1-\tfrac{j}{n^{3}}\bigr)}
            {n\sqrt{\,1+\tfrac{j}{n^{2}}\,}}
       +\mathcal{O}(n^{4}).
\]
Since $\tfrac{j}{n^{2}}=sn\ge 0$,  
\[
\sqrt{\,1+\tfrac{j}{n^{2}}\,}
     =\sqrt{1+sn}
     =\sqrt{sn}\Bigl(1+\frac{1}{sn}\Bigr)^{1/2}
     =\sqrt{sn}+\mathcal{O}(n^{-1/2}),
\]
so
\[
\frac{n^{3}-j}{\sqrt{n^{2}+j}}
     =n^{3/2}\frac{1-s}{\sqrt{s}}
      +\mathcal{O}(n^{1/2}).
\]

Turn the sum into a Riemann integral:
\[
\begin{aligned}
T_{n}^{(3)}
   &=n^{3/2}\sum_{j=1}^{n^{3}}
          \frac{1-\tfrac{j}{n^{3}}}{\sqrt{\tfrac{j}{n^{3}}}}
          +\mathcal{O}(n^{4})                              \\[2mm]
   &=n^{3/2}\,n^{3}\int_{0}^{1}\frac{1-s}{\sqrt{s}}\,ds
      +\mathcal{O}(n^{4})                                 \\[2mm]
   &=n^{9/2}\!\int_{0}^{1}(s^{-1/2}-s^{1/2})\,ds
      +\mathcal{O}(n^{4})                                 \\[2mm]
   &=n^{9/2}\Bigl(2-\frac{2}{3}\Bigr)+\mathcal{O}(n^{4})
    =\frac{4}{3}\,n^{9/2}+\mathcal{O}(n^{4}).
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\boxed{\;
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{T_{n}^{(3)}}{n^{9/2}}=\frac{4}{3}
\;},\qquad
T_{n}^{(3)}\sim\frac{4}{3}\,n^{9/2},
\]
and in particular $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}T_{n}^{(3)}=+\infty$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
â€˘ Dimensional escalation â€“ Part (a) involves a 3-fold rather than a single sum, forcing
  the passage to a triple integral and use of multivariable calculus
  (polar coordinates, Î˛â€“functions).

â€˘ Oscillatory behaviour plus a non-separable kernel â€“ Part (b) mixes trigonometric
  factors with a quadratic denominator that couples the two discrete variables.
  One must combine Riemann-sum arguments, integration in two variables,
  and non-elementary antiderivatives.

â€˘ Nested sums with quadratic growth â€“ Part (c) requires counting arguments and
  asymptotic estimates on a double sum whose length itself depends on the outer
  index, a step beyond the oneâ€“dimensional bounding used in the original kernel.

Together these raise the problem from one-dimensional Riemann-sum
manipulations to a setting that simultaneously employs higher-dimensional
analysis, special integral evaluations, and growth estimates for
multi-level sumsâ€”substantially more sophisticated than either the original
exercise or the current kernel variant.
